ops.title: A reproduced finding outranks an authority's contradiction — make them prove it, don't soften it
ops.body:
When an orchestrator, reviewer, or the user contradicts a finding you have *reproduced*, do not walk it back. Restate the reproduction and ask them to check their own premise.

**Why:** In the experiment-at-home `athome.train` fix (2026-07-14), I reported that `.python-version` pinned `3.14`, not `3.14t`, so the workspace venv was GIL-enabled and the free-threading gate could not pass. The team lead pushed back — their venv *was* free-threaded and CI was green — and I softened it to "my workspace resolved wrong." I was right and the lead was wrong: main's file really did say `3.14`, and their venv was free-threaded only by historical accident, predating the pin. A fresh clone got a GIL-enabled interpreter, so the free-threading contract the package exists to honor was being met by luck. Their verbatim correction: *"Don't defer next time just because I push — make me prove it."*

**How to apply:** The tell is a claim backed by a *minimal reproduction* — mine was a probe with zero project imports dying at interpreter startup, which isolated the interpreter from the code. That evidence does not weaken because someone senior disagrees; their contradicting observation is a second data point to reconcile, not a refutation. Say "here is the exact command and output — check your copy of the file" and hold. An authority's environment is often the *least* representative one (long-lived, mutated, predates the config it appears to satisfy). Related: [[fix-tooling-breakage-dont-punt]] — a red check you run is yours to fix, and "unrelated" is not a resolution.
